text: Maps are among humanity's oldest tools for making sense of space, distilling the sprawling complexity of the world into something we can hold in our hands or call up on a screen. Every map is an argument as much as a description: by choosing what to include, what to omit, and how to project a curved earth onto a flat surface, the mapmaker shapes how we understand a place before we ever set foot in it. They serve countless purposes, guiding travelers, marking boundaries, and recording the distribution of everything from rainfall to languages to disease. Yet for all their usefulness, maps are never neutral or complete; as the saying goes, the map is not the territory, and the gap between the two is where both their power and their potential to mislead reside.
